summary: Jeff Atwood argues that the web browser address bar has evolved into a modern command line interface, powered by search engines rather than local operating systems. Chrome's approach of passing non-URI input to search engines enables powerful query capabilities including weather, calculations, conversions, and stock quotes. He notes that Google's search operators function similarly to UNIX command line parameters, while JavaScript bookmarklets provide scripting capabilities. Services like yubnub further extend this by enabling collaborative, crowdsourced command creation. Atwood concludes that despite historically preferring GUIs, the search-oriented, publicly accessible command prompt may represent the future of computing interfaces.
key_points: Browser address bars now function as command lines by passing non-URL input to search engines
Google's search features (weather, calculations, conversions, stock quotes) mirror traditional command line utility functions
Advanced search operators like filetype:, site:, and daterange: parallel UNIX command line parameters
JavaScript bookmarklets enable scripting capabilities within the browser address bar
Services like yubnub create a collaborative, crowdsourced command system that extends browser capabilities socially
money_quotes: I like to think of the web browser address bar as the new command line.
While web browsers should have some built-in smarts, they can never match the collective intelligence of a worldwide search engine.
Any command line worth its salt has some kind of scripting language built in, too, right?
Apparently we've spent the last 20 years reimplementing the UNIX command line in the browser.
Perhaps the command prompt – or more specifically, the search oriented, crowdsourced, world public command prompt – really is the future.
I was never a big command-line enthusiast; even way back when on my Amiga I'd choose the GUI over the CLI whenever I could. But maybe I bet on the wrong horse.
key_insight: The browser address bar, powered by search engines and collaborative services, has become a globally accessible, crowdsourced command line that surpasses traditional CLIs by leveraging collective intelligence rather than local knowledge.
